Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 3 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes CHAPTER 1 Foundation of Large-Scale Systems Design 1.1 What is System Design? System design is the process of defining the architecture, components, modules, interfaces, and

the process of defining the architecture, components, modules, interfaces, and data flow of a system to satisfy specified requirements. Large-scale systems must handle millions of users, petabytes of data, and high concurrency while remaining reliable and maintainable.  Examples of large-scale systems: Google Search (8.5 billion queries/day), Netflix (270M subscribers),

large-scale systems: Google Search (8.5 billion queries/day), Netflix (270M subscribers), WhatsApp (100B messages/day). 1.2 System Design Principles Scalability The ability to handle increasing load by adding resources (scale up = bigger machine; scale out = more machines). Reliability The system continues to work correctly even when faults occur. Measured by

continues to work correctly even when faults occur. Measured by MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures). Availability The fraction of time the system is operational. Formula: Availability = Uptime / (Uptime + Downtime). 99.9% = ~8.7 hrs downtime/year. Maintainability How easy it is to fix bugs, add features, or operate the

it is to fix bugs, add features, or operate the system. Achieved through modularity, good documentation, and clean code. Performance How fast the system responds to requests (latency) and how much work it does per unit time (throughput). Fault Tolerance The system's ability to continue operating despite partial failures by

The system's ability to continue operating despite partial failures by using redundancy, replication, and failover. Consistency All nodes see the same data at the same time. Strong consistency is hard to achieve in distributed systems. Security Protection against unauthorized access, data breaches, and denial-of-service attacks. 1.3 Functional vs Non-Functional Requirements

data breaches, and denial-of-service attacks. 1.3 Functional vs Non-Functional Requirements Aspect Functional Requirements Non-Functional Requirements Definition WHAT the system should do HOW WELL the system should do it Nature Business logic and features Quality attributes and constraints Examples User can post a tweet, search for items, make a payment Response
post a tweet, search for items, make a payment Response in < 200ms, 99.99% uptime, handle 1M concurrent users Testability Verified by checking if feature works Verified by load testing, benchmarking

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 4 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes Changes Can change with business needs Often fixed by SLAs/architecture choices ■ INTERVIEW TIP: In a design interview: always clarify BOTH functional AND non-functional requirements

a design interview: always clarify BOTH functional AND non-functional requirements before designing. Non-functional requirements drive the entire architecture. 1.4 Latency vs Throughput Latency is the time taken to complete a single request (measured in ms or seconds). Throughput is the number of requests processed per unit time (measured in req/sec

number of requests processed per unit time (measured in req/sec or QPS — Queries Per Second). Concept Latency Throughput Analogy Time for one car to travel from A to B Number of cars passing a point per hour Unit Milliseconds (ms), seconds Requests/sec, Queries/sec (QPS), MB/s Goal Minimize — lower

(ms), seconds Requests/sec, Queries/sec (QPS), MB/s Goal Minimize — lower is better Maximize — higher is better Bottleneck Network delays, slow database queries CPU bound, I/O bound, bandwidth Improve by Caching, CDN, faster DB queries Horizontal scaling, async processing, batching Trade-off High throughput can sometimes increase p99 latency Low latency

Trade-off High throughput can sometimes increase p99 latency Low latency can limit throughput if resources are limited ■ MUST KNOW: Latency numbers every engineer should know: L1 cache = 1ns, RAM = 100ns, SSD = 100 microseconds, HDD = 10ms, Network (same region) = 0.5ms, Network (cross-continent) = 150ms. 1.5

Network (same region) = 0.5ms, Network (cross-continent) = 150ms. 1.5 Design Approaches Top-Down vs Bottom-Up Approach Description When to Use Top-Down Start with high-level architecture and decompose into components System design interviews, new system design Bottom-Up Start with components/services and compose them into a system Building on existing microservices, iterative

compose them into a system Building on existing microservices, iterative development 1.6 Architectural Patterns Monolithic All components in one deployable unit. Simple to develop initially, but hard to scale and maintain as it grows. Example: Traditional e-commerce apps. Microservices Application split into small, independent services, each responsible for one business
split into small, independent services, each responsible for one business capability. Each can be deployed, scaled, and updated independently. Example: Netflix (700+ microservices).

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 5 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes Event-Driven Components communicate through events/messages. Decoupled, asynchronous. Example: Kafka-based pipelines, real-time analytics. Layered (N-Tier) System divided into layers: Presentation, Business Logic, Data. Each layer talks

divided into layers: Presentation, Business Logic, Data. Each layer talks only to adjacent layers. Example: Traditional web apps. CQRS Command Query Responsibility Segregation — separate read and write paths for scalability and performance. Event Sourcing Store all changes as a sequence of events, not current state. The current state is

sequence of events, not current state. The current state is derived by replaying events. Service Mesh Infrastructure layer handling service-to-service communication (load balancing, service discovery, observability). Example: Istio, Linkerd. ■ KEY TAKEAWAYS ✓ Functional = what system does; Non-Functional = how well (availability, latency, throughput). ✓ Latency = single request

how well (availability, latency, throughput). ✓ Latency = single request time; Throughput = requests per second. ✓ Availability 99.9% = 8.7 hrs downtime/year; 99.99% = 52 min; 99.999% = 5 min. ✓ Monolith = single unit; Microservices = independent services; Event-Driven = async messages. ✓ Always gather BOTH types of
Event-Driven = async messages. ✓ Always gather BOTH types of requirements before designing.

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 6 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes CHAPTER 2 Fundamentals of Distributed Systems 2.1 Definition & Characteristics A distributed system is a collection of independent computers that appears to its users as

collection of independent computers that appears to its users as a single coherent system. The computers communicate and coordinate only by passing messages over a network. Leslie Lamport's famous quote: "A distributed system is one in which the failure of a computer you didn't even know existed can render your

a computer you didn't even know existed can render your own computer unusable." Concurrency Multiple components execute simultaneously. Requires careful coordination to avoid race conditions and data inconsistency. No Global Clock Each machine has its own clock. There is no single global time — events can't be ordered by wall-clock

single global time — events can't be ordered by wall-clock time alone. Requires logical clocks (Lamport, Vector). Partial Failures Some components can fail while others continue working. The system must detect and handle partial failures gracefully. Message Passing Communication only happens through message passing over the network — which can

happens through message passing over the network — which can be slow, unreliable, or reordered. Transparency The system hides the complexity of distribution from users: access, location, migration, replication, and failure transparency. 2.2 Advantages of Distributed Systems  Scalability: Add more machines to handle growing load (horizontal scaling) — virtually

more machines to handle growing load (horizontal scaling) — virtually unlimited capacity.  Fault Tolerance: No single point of failure — if one node fails, others continue serving requests.  Performance: Work can be parallelized across many machines; data can be served from geographically closer nodes (reduced latency).  Resource

be served from geographically closer nodes (reduced latency).  Resource Sharing: Hardware, data, and software can be shared across the network.  Availability: Replicated data and services ensure the system is always accessible. 2.3 Challenges of Distributed Systems  Network Unreliability: Messages can be lost, delayed, duplicated, or arrive out

Unreliability: Messages can be lost, delayed, duplicated, or arrive out of order.  Partial Failures: Hard to distinguish a slow node from a crashed node.  Consistency vs Availability: Maintaining data consistency across replicas while staying highly available is fundamentally difficult (CAP Theorem).  Security: Larger attack surface; messages can

difficult (CAP Theorem).  Security: Larger attack surface; messages can be intercepted.  Complexity: Distributed systems are much harder to build, test, and debug than centralized systems.  Clock Skew: No global clock — ordering events across nodes is non-trivial. 2.4 Types of Distributed Systems Type Description Examples
2.4 Types of Distributed Systems Type Description Examples

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 7 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes Distributed Computing Systems Cluster/Grid/Cloud computing for large computations Google MapReduce, Apache Spark, AWS EMR Distributed Information Systems Replicated databases, transaction processing Distributed databases: Cassandra, CockroachDB

Information Systems Replicated databases, transaction processing Distributed databases: Cassandra, CockroachDB Distributed Pervasive Systems IoT, mobile networks, sensor networks Smart home devices, vehicle sensor networks 2.5 Client-Server vs Peer-to-Peer Models Feature Client-Server Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Architecture Dedicated server(s) + many clients Every node is both client and server Control Centralized — server

node is both client and server Control Centralized — server controls resources Decentralized — no central authority Scalability Server can become bottleneck Scales naturally — more peers = more resources Fault Tolerance Single server failure = service down No single point of failure Consistency Easier — server is authoritative source

point of failure Consistency Easier — server is authoritative source Harder — must sync across peers Examples Web (HTTP), Email (SMTP), DNS, FTP BitTorrent, Blockchain, Gnutella, IPFS Security Easier to secure (single entry point) Harder to secure (open network) Architecture Diagrams CLIENT-SERVER: PEER-TO-PEER: [Client] [Peer A] <---> [Peer B] [Client]

Diagrams CLIENT-SERVER: PEER-TO-PEER: [Client] [Peer A] <---> [Peer B] [Client] -----> [Server] ^ ^ [Client] | | [Client] [Peer D] <---> [Peer C] ■ KEY TAKEAWAYS ✓ Distributed system: independent computers appearing as one system, communicating by messages. ✓ Key characteristics: Concurrency, No Global Clock, Partial Failures, Message Passing. ✓

characteristics: Concurrency, No Global Clock, Partial Failures, Message Passing. ✓ Client-Server: centralized, server authoritative; P2P: decentralized, every node equal. ✓ Main challenges: network unreliability, partial failures, consistency vs availability.

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 8 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes CHAPTER 3 Distributed System Failures 3.1 Why Failures are Inevitable In a large distributed system with 1,000 nodes, if each node has 99.9% uptime, the

with 1,000 nodes, if each node has 99.9% uptime, the probability of ALL nodes being up at the same time is 0.999^1000 = 36.8%. So failures are the norm, not the exception! 3.2 Failure Models Crash Failure A node simply stops working and does not respond to further messages. The

stops working and does not respond to further messages. The simplest failure model — the node is either working or completely stopped. Example: Server power failure. Omission Failure A node fails to send or receive some messages. Send omission: node fails to send a message. Receive omission: node fails to

fails to send a message. Receive omission: node fails to receive a message. Example: Network packet drops. Timing Failure A node delivers responses outside the specified time bounds — too slow (late) or in some cases too fast. Example: Slow database query causing timeout. Byzantine Failure The worst case —

database query causing timeout. Byzantine Failure The worst case — a node behaves arbitrarily and may send INCORRECT, inconsistent, or malicious data to different nodes. Named after the Byzantine Generals Problem. Example: Hacked node, bit-flip errors. Network Partition The network splits into two or more groups that cannot communicate with

splits into two or more groups that cannot communicate with each other, even though individual nodes are still functioning. Example: Data centre network switch failure. Fail-Stop Failure A node crashes and others can DETECT the crash. Idealised version of crash failure — easier to handle. ■ ORDER: Failure Severity Order

failure — easier to handle. ■ ORDER: Failure Severity Order (easiest to handle → hardest): Crash Stop < Crash < Omission < Timing < Byzantine 3.3 Fault Tolerance Strategies Redundancy  Active Redundancy (Hot Standby): Multiple replicas process the same request simultaneously. If primary fails, standby takes over immediately with

request simultaneously. If primary fails, standby takes over immediately with zero downtime.  Passive Redundancy (Warm Standby): Backup receives updates but is not processing live traffic. Takes seconds to failover.  Cold Standby: Backup only starts up when primary fails. Cheapest but longest failover time. Replication  Purpose: Store copies

Cheapest but longest failover time. Replication  Purpose: Store copies of data on multiple nodes to prevent data loss and allow reads from nearest replica.  Synchronous Replication: Write is committed only after ALL replicas acknowledge. Strong consistency, but higher latency.
latency.

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 9 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes  Asynchronous Replication: Write is committed after primary acknowledges; replicas update eventually. Lower latency, but risk of data loss. Checkpointing  Periodically save the state

risk of data loss. Checkpointing  Periodically save the state of the system so that on failure, it can restart from the last checkpoint instead of from scratch. Timeouts & Retries  If a message doesn't get a response within a timeout period, assume failure and retry or redirect. 

a timeout period, assume failure and retry or redirect.  Exponential Backoff: Retry after 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s... to avoid thundering herd problem.  Circuit Breaker Pattern: After N failures, stop sending requests to a failing service to give it time to recover. ■ KEY TAKEAWAYS ✓ 6 failure types:

time to recover. ■ KEY TAKEAWAYS ✓ 6 failure types: Crash, Omission, Timing, Byzantine, Network Partition, Fail-Stop. ✓ Byzantine failure is worst — node sends arbitrary/incorrect data (can be malicious). ✓ Fault tolerance strategies: Redundancy, Replication, Checkpointing, Timeouts, Circuit Breaker. ✓ Synchronous replication = strong consistency but slow; Async =
✓ Synchronous replication = strong consistency but slow; Async = fast but possible data loss.

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 10 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes CHAPTER 4 Process Coordination & Synchronization 4.1 The Need for Coordination In a distributed system, multiple processes run concurrently across different nodes. They must coordinate

multiple processes run concurrently across different nodes. They must coordinate to: (1) agree on a shared state, (2) access shared resources without conflict, and (3) handle failures gracefully. 4.2 Logical Clocks — Ordering Events The Problem In distributed systems, there's no global clock. Two events on different machines cannot be

no global clock. Two events on different machines cannot be compared using wall-clock time due to clock skew (clocks running at different speeds). We need a way to determine the order of events. Lamport Timestamps (1978) Leslie Lamport introduced logical clocks to order events. Each process maintains a counter: 

clocks to order events. Each process maintains a counter:  Rule 1: Before executing an event, increment your counter: L = L + 1  Rule 2: When sending a message, include your current timestamp.  Rule 3: When receiving a message with timestamp T: L = max(L, T) +

a message with timestamp T: L = max(L, T) + 1 This gives us: If A happened-before B (A → B), then L(A) < L(B). But L(A) < L(B) does NOT necessarily mean A → B (can't detect concurrent events). Vector Clocks Each process maintains a vector of counters —

Vector Clocks Each process maintains a vector of counters — one per process. Vector clocks can detect concurrent events (neither A → B nor B → A).  Each process Pi maintains V[i] = [0, 0, ..., 0] (length N for N processes).  On internal event: increment V[i][i] 

for N processes).  On internal event: increment V[i][i]  On send: increment V[i][i], attach V[i] to message  On receive from Pj: V[i][k] = max(V[i][k], V[j][k]) for all k, then increment V[i][i] 4.3 Mutual Exclusion Mutual exclusion ensures that only ONE process accesses a critical section (shared resource) at

only ONE process accesses a critical section (shared resource) at a time. This prevents race conditions in distributed systems. Algorithm How it Works Messages/Entry Pros Cons Centralized One coordinator grants/denies lock requests 3 messages Simple, fair Single point of failure, bottleneck
bottleneck

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 11 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes Ricart-Agrawala Multicast request; wait for OK from ALL others; uses timestamps to break ties 2(N-1) messages No central coordinator High message overhead; one failure blocks

messages No central coordinator High message overhead; one failure blocks all Token Ring Processes arranged in logical ring; only token holder enters critical section 1 to N messages Fair, bounded wait Lost token is a problem Maekawa's Algorithm Quorum-based — only need OK from a subset (quorum) of processes O(sqrt(N))

only need OK from a subset (quorum) of processes O(sqrt(N)) Fewer messages than Ricart-Agrawala Complex quorum management 4.4 Deadlock Handling A deadlock occurs when two or more processes are waiting for each other to release resources, and none can proceed. Four Necessary Conditions (Coffman Conditions)  Mutual Exclusion: At least

Four Necessary Conditions (Coffman Conditions)  Mutual Exclusion: At least one resource is held in non-sharable mode.  Hold and Wait: A process holding resources is waiting for additional resources held by others.  No Preemption: Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a process.  Circular Wait: A circular chain

taken from a process.  Circular Wait: A circular chain of processes exists, each waiting for a resource held by the next. Deadlock Strategies Strategy Approach Mechanism Prevention Eliminate one Coffman condition Total resource ordering; request all resources at once Avoidance Allocate resources only if safe state maintained Banker's Algorithm

Avoidance Allocate resources only if safe state maintained Banker's Algorithm — check if allocation leads to safe state Detection Allow deadlock, detect it, then recover Maintain resource allocation graph (RAG); look for cycles Recovery After detection, break the deadlock Kill a process; preempt (rollback) a resource; restart Distributed Deadlock Detection

a process; preempt (rollback) a resource; restart Distributed Deadlock Detection  Wait-For Graph (WFG): Edge from Pi to Pj means Pi is waiting for Pj to release a resource. A cycle in WFG = deadlock.  Chandy-Misra-Haas Algorithm: Probe messages are sent along WFG edges; if a probe returns to
are sent along WFG edges; if a probe returns to the initiator, a deadlock is detected.

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 12 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes ■ KEY TAKEAWAYS ✓ Lamport clocks: L = max(L, received_timestamp) + 1. Capture happened-before, not concurrency. ✓ Vector clocks: detect concurrent events; V[i][k] = max(V[i][k],

concurrency. ✓ Vector clocks: detect concurrent events; V[i][k] = max(V[i][k], V[j][k]) on receive. ✓ Mutual exclusion algorithms: Centralized (3 msg), Ricart-Agrawala (2(N-1) msg), Token Ring. ✓ Deadlock = Mutual Exclusion + Hold&Wait; + No Preemption + Circular Wait (ALL 4 needed). ✓ Deadlock handling: Prevention / Avoidance (Banker's) / Detection
needed). ✓ Deadlock handling: Prevention / Avoidance (Banker's) / Detection (WFG) / Recovery.

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 13 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes CHAPTER 5 Parallel Computing vs Distributed Computing 5.1 Parallel Computing Parallel computing is the simultaneous use of multiple compute resources (processors/cores) to solve a problem.

use of multiple compute resources (processors/cores) to solve a problem. The problem is divided into smaller parts that execute concurrently on a single machine or tightly coupled cluster.  Goal: Speed up computation by doing many things at the same time.  Memory: Processors typically share memory (shared-memory model). 

time.  Memory: Processors typically share memory (shared-memory model).  Coupling: Tightly coupled — low-latency communication via shared memory bus.  Example: Matrix multiplication using multi-threading; GPU computing with CUDA; rendering frames in parallel. Flynn's Taxonomy (Parallel Architectures) Type Instruction Streams Data Streams Example SISD Single Single Traditional single-core CPU

Streams Data Streams Example SISD Single Single Traditional single-core CPU SIMD Single Multiple GPU, vector processors, AVX instructions MISD Multiple Single Rare — fault-tolerant flight computers MIMD Multiple Multiple Modern multi-core CPUs, computer clusters Amdahl's Law Amdahl's Law predicts the theoretical speedup from parallelizing a program: Speedup = 1 /

theoretical speedup from parallelizing a program: Speedup = 1 / (S + (1-S)/N) Where S = sequential fraction of the program (cannot be parallelized), N = number of processors. ■ EXAMPLE: If 20% of code is sequential (S=0.2) and N=100 processors: Max Speedup = 1 / (0.2 + 0.8/100) =

processors: Max Speedup = 1 / (0.2 + 0.8/100) = 4.95x. Even with infinite processors, speedup is limited to 1/S = 5x. 5.2 Distributed Computing Distributed computing uses multiple independent computers (nodes) connected by a network to solve problems collaboratively. Each node has its own memory and communicates only by

Each node has its own memory and communicates only by passing messages.  Goal: Achieve scale, fault tolerance, and geographic distribution.  Memory: Each node has private memory — no shared memory.  Coupling: Loosely coupled — high-latency network communication.  Example: Google MapReduce, Apache Spark, blockchain networks.
 Example: Google MapReduce, Apache Spark, blockchain networks.

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 14 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes 5.3 Parallel vs Distributed — Detailed Comparison Feature Parallel Computing Distributed Computing Location Same machine (shared or distributed memory) Different machines connected by network Memory

(shared or distributed memory) Different machines connected by network Memory Often shared (UMA/NUMA) Private per node (no shared memory) Communication Shared memory or low-latency bus Message passing over network (high latency) Failure Model If one processor fails, whole program may fail Partial failures — some nodes fail, others continue Coordination

fail Partial failures — some nodes fail, others continue Coordination OS/hardware level synchronization Distributed protocols (consensus, leader election) Scalability Limited by single machine capacity Can scale to thousands of nodes globally Goal Speed — reduce computation time Scale + Fault Tolerance + Geographic distribution Clock Single global clock (or synchronized)

Tolerance + Geographic distribution Clock Single global clock (or synchronized) No global clock — logical clocks needed Examples Multi-threading, CUDA, OpenMP, MPI on cluster Hadoop, Spark, Cassandra, Kafka, Kubernetes ■ COMBINED SYSTEMS: A system can be BOTH parallel AND distributed: e.g., Apache Spark runs distributed across many machines (distributed), and

e.g., Apache Spark runs distributed across many machines (distributed), and each machine uses multi-core parallel processing (parallel). ■ KEY TAKEAWAYS ✓ Parallel: same machine, speed up by simultaneous execution, often shared memory. ✓ Distributed: different machines, communicate by messages, scale + fault tolerance. ✓ Amdahl's Law: speedup limited by sequential

+ fault tolerance. ✓ Amdahl's Law: speedup limited by sequential fraction S — max = 1/S even with infinite CPUs. ✓ Flynn's Taxonomy: SISD, SIMD, MISD, MIMD — MIMD = most common today (multi-core CPU). ✓ Key difference: distributed systems have partial failures and no global clock.
have partial failures and no global clock.

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 15 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes CHAPTER 6 CAP Theorem & PACELC Theorem 6.1 CAP Theorem CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer, 2000; formally proven by Gilbert & Lynch, 2002) states that a

formally proven by Gilbert & Lynch, 2002) states that a distributed system can guarantee AT MOST TWO of the following three properties simultaneously: C — Consistency Every read receives the most recent write or an error. All nodes see the same data at the same time. (Note: NOT the same

same data at the same time. (Note: NOT the same as ACID consistency.) A — Availability Every request receives a non-error response — but it might not contain the most recent data. The system is always responsive. P — Partition Tolerance The system continues operating even when network partitions (communication

Tolerance The system continues operating even when network partitions (communication breaks between nodes) occur. CAP Theorem Triangle CONSISTENCY / \ / \ (CA) / \ (CP) RDBMS / CAN \ MongoDB Postgres / ONLY \ HBase / PICK 2! \ / \ AVAILABILITY _______________PARTITION TOLERANCE (AP) Cassandra, CouchDB, DynamoDB Choice

/ \ AVAILABILITY _______________PARTITION TOLERANCE (AP) Cassandra, CouchDB, DynamoDB Choice Guarantee Sacrifice When to Choose Examples CP Consistent + Partition Tolerant Availability — may reject requests during partition Banking, financial transactions, where wrong data is worse than no data MongoDB (primary-based), HBase, Zookeeper AP Available + Partition Tolerant Consistency —

(primary-based), HBase, Zookeeper AP Available + Partition Tolerant Consistency — may return stale data Social media, shopping carts, DNS, where availability matters more than exactness Cassandra, CouchDB, DynamoDB, Riak CA Consistent + Available Partition Tolerance — fails during network partition Single-node or LAN systems where network never partitions (theoretical) Traditional
Single-node or LAN systems where network never partitions (theoretical) Traditional RDBMS on single node

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 16 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes ■ IMPORTANT: In practice, network partitions DO happen (you can't prevent them). So real systems must choose between CP and AP — CA is not

must choose between CP and AP — CA is not viable for distributed systems over an unreliable network. 6.2 Consistency Models Consistency is not binary — it's a spectrum. From strongest to weakest: Strong Consistency (Linearizability) After a write completes, all subsequent reads from any node return the new value.

all subsequent reads from any node return the new value. Operations appear instantaneous and ordered. Highest guarantee, highest latency. Used in: banking, Zookeeper. Sequential Consistency All nodes see operations in the same order, but that order doesn't have to match real time. Weaker than linearizability. Causal Consistency Causally related operations

real time. Weaker than linearizability. Causal Consistency Causally related operations (A caused B) are seen in order by all nodes. Concurrent operations may be seen in any order. Used in: distributed collaborative editing. Eventual Consistency If no new updates are made, all replicas will eventually converge to the same value.

made, all replicas will eventually converge to the same value. No guarantee of when. Used in: DNS, Cassandra, DynamoDB defaults, social media feeds. Read-Your-Writes A process always reads its own latest write. Doesn't guarantee other processes see it. Used in: user profile updates. Monotonic Read Once a process reads a

user profile updates. Monotonic Read Once a process reads a value, it never reads an older value in subsequent reads. 6.3 PACELC Theorem The PACELC theorem (Daniel Abadi, 2010) extends CAP by also considering the trade-off in the normal case (no partition): PACELC = PAC + ELC  P —

(no partition): PACELC = PAC + ELC  P — Partition: If there's a network partition...  A — Availability vs C — Consistency: ...choose Availability or Consistency (= CAP).  E — Else (normal operation, no partition)...  L — Latency vs C — Consistency: ...choose between low Latency

— Latency vs C — Consistency: ...choose between low Latency or strong Consistency. System Partition Choice (P→A/C) Normal Case Choice (E→L/C) Category DynamoDB PA (Availability) EL (Low Latency) PA/EL — Highly available, eventually consistent Cassandra PA (Availability) EL (Low Latency) PA/EL — Tunable consistency Zookeeper PC (Consistency) EC (Consistency) PC/EC
PA/EL — Tunable consistency Zookeeper PC (Consistency) EC (Consistency) PC/EC — Strong consistency, higher latency MongoDB PC (Consistency) EC (Consistency) PC/EC — Default strong consistency

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 17 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes PNUTS (Yahoo) PA (Availability) EC (Consistency) PA/EC — Mixed: available in partition, consistent otherwise ■ KEY TAKEAWAYS ✓ CAP: Consistency + Availability + Partition Tolerance

KEY TAKEAWAYS ✓ CAP: Consistency + Availability + Partition Tolerance — can only guarantee 2 of 3. ✓ In real distributed systems: network partitions WILL happen, so choose CP or AP. ✓ CP examples: MongoDB, Zookeeper, HBase. AP examples: Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB. ✓ Consistency spectrum: Strong (Linearizable) > Sequential >
DynamoDB, CouchDB. ✓ Consistency spectrum: Strong (Linearizable) > Sequential > Causal > Eventual. ✓ PACELC adds: during normal operation, trade-off is Latency vs Consistency.

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 18 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes CHAPTER 7 Consensus Algorithms: Raft & Paxos 7.1 What is Consensus? Consensus is the problem of getting multiple nodes in a distributed system to agree

of getting multiple nodes in a distributed system to agree on a single value (e.g., who the leader is, what the next log entry is). This is fundamental to building reliable distributed systems.  Safety: All correct nodes agree on the SAME value.  Liveness: Eventually, a value IS decided

the SAME value.  Liveness: Eventually, a value IS decided (the system makes progress).  FLP Impossibility (1985): It is impossible to achieve consensus in an asynchronous system if even one process can fail. In practice, systems use timeouts to work around this. 7.2 Paxos — Internal Working Paxos (Leslie

work around this. 7.2 Paxos — Internal Working Paxos (Leslie Lamport, 1989) is the foundational consensus algorithm. It tolerates crash failures (not Byzantine). Roles in Paxos  Proposer: Proposes a value (initiates consensus).  Acceptor: Votes on proposals (core of the algorithm). Typically a quorum of N/2+1 acceptors is needed.

the algorithm). Typically a quorum of N/2+1 acceptors is needed.  Learner: Learns the chosen value after consensus is reached. Paxos Phases Basic Paxos Algorithm Flow PHASE 1 — PREPARE: Proposer selects proposal number n and sends Prepare(n) to majority of Acceptors. Each Acceptor: if n > highest seen, promise

of Acceptors. Each Acceptor: if n > highest seen, promise to not accept proposals < n, reply with Promise(n, highest_accepted_value). PHASE 2 — ACCEPT: If Proposer receives promises from majority: - Use highest accepted value from promises (if any), else use own value. - Send Accept(n, value) to acceptors. Each

use own value. - Send Accept(n, value) to acceptors. Each Acceptor: if n >= promised, accept the value, send Accepted(n, value). PHASE 3 — LEARN: If majority sent Accepted, consensus is reached. Notify Learners. ■ MULTI-PAXOS: Multi-Paxos: Optimizes Paxos for multiple sequential decisions (like a replicated log) by electing a

multiple sequential decisions (like a replicated log) by electing a stable leader, so Phase 1 only needs to happen once, then Phase 2 repeats for each log entry. 7.3 Raft — Internal Working Raft (Ongaro & Ousterhout, 2014) was designed to be MORE UNDERSTANDABLE than Paxos while being equivalent in
to be MORE UNDERSTANDABLE than Paxos while being equivalent in fault tolerance. Used in: etcd, CockroachDB, TiKV, Consul. Key Concepts

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 19 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes Server States Each Raft node is in one of 3 states: FOLLOWER (default, receives updates), CANDIDATE (campaigns for leadership), LEADER (handles all writes and replicates

CANDIDATE (campaigns for leadership), LEADER (handles all writes and replicates log). Term Raft divides time into terms (logical clock). Each term begins with an election. Terms are numbered monotonically. If a node sees a higher term, it immediately becomes a follower. Log A replicated log of commands. Leader appends entries

follower. Log A replicated log of commands. Leader appends entries and replicates to followers. Entry is committed when replicated to majority. Quorum Majority of nodes (floor(N/2)+1). Any two quorums share at least one member — this ensures consistency. Raft — Leader Election Raft Leader Election NORMAL STATE: ELECTION TRIGGER: [Leader]

Leader Election Raft Leader Election NORMAL STATE: ELECTION TRIGGER: [Leader] --heartbeat--> [Follower] Follower receives no heartbeat within [Leader] --heartbeat--> [Follower] election timeout (150-300ms random). [Leader] --heartbeat--> [Follower] Follower becomes CANDIDATE. ELECTION PROCESS: 1. Candidate increments term, votes for itself. 2. Sends RequestVote(term, candidateId, lastLogIndex, lastLogTerm) to all. 3. Each node

Sends RequestVote(term, candidateId, lastLogIndex, lastLogTerm) to all. 3. Each node grants vote if: (a) hasn't voted this term, AND (b) candidate's log is at least as up-to-date as voter's log. 4. Candidate with majority votes becomes LEADER. 5. New leader immediately sends heartbeats to assert authority. Raft — Log Replication

immediately sends heartbeats to assert authority. Raft — Log Replication  Step 1: Client sends command to Leader.  Step 2: Leader appends command to its log as a new entry.  Step 3: Leader sends AppendEntries RPC to all followers.  Step 4: When majority of followers acknowledge, leader

followers.  Step 4: When majority of followers acknowledge, leader COMMITS the entry.  Step 5: Leader applies to state machine and responds to client. Next heartbeat informs followers of commit. 7.4 Raft vs Paxos Comparison Feature Raft Paxos Understandability Designed for understandability — strong leader model is intuitive Notoriously

Designed for understandability — strong leader model is intuitive Notoriously complex and hard to understand/implement Leader Strong single leader for all writes; election is simple Paxos can have multiple proposers causing conflicts (Multi-Paxos solves this) Log Explicit log structure is central to algorithm Paxos basic form doesn't define log; Multi-Paxos
central to algorithm Paxos basic form doesn't define log; Multi-Paxos adds it Membership change Joint consensus approach for cluster membership changes Not well-specified in original paper

Used in etcd, CockroachDB, TiKV, Consul, RethinkDB Google Chubby/Spanner, Apache Zookeeper (ZAB is Paxos-like) ■ KEY TAKEAWAYS ✓ Consensus: agree on a single value — Safety (all agree same value) + Liveness (progress). ✓ FLP Impossibility: cannot solve consensus in async system with even 1 possible failure. ✓ Paxos phases:

async system with even 1 possible failure. ✓ Paxos phases: Prepare → Promise → Accept → Accepted. Need quorum (majority) at each phase. ✓ Raft states: Follower → Candidate → Leader. Uses terms and heartbeats for leader election. ✓ Raft: majority commit, randomized election timeout prevents split votes. ✓ Both
majority commit, randomized election timeout prevents split votes. ✓ Both Raft and Paxos tolerate f failures with 2f+1 nodes.

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 21 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes CHAPTER 8 Leader Election & Failure Detection 8.1 Why Leader Election? Many distributed algorithms require a coordinator or leader — a single process that has

a coordinator or leader — a single process that has special responsibilities (e.g., accepting writes, coordinating transactions, deciding log entries). When a leader fails, the system must elect a new leader automatically to continue functioning. 8.2 Leader Election Algorithms Bully Algorithm The Bully Algorithm assumes each node has a unique

Algorithm The Bully Algorithm assumes each node has a unique ID. The node with the highest ID becomes leader. Bully Algorithm SCENARIO: Node 3 detects Node 5 (current leader) has failed. Step 1: Node 3 sends ELECTION message to all nodes with higher IDs (4, 5, 6, 7). Step 2:

nodes with higher IDs (4, 5, 6, 7). Step 2: If no response within timeout => Node 3 declares itself leader. If Node 4, 6, or 7 responds with OK: Step 3: Responding node starts its OWN election (sends ELECTION to nodes above it). Step 4: Node with highest ID

to nodes above it). Step 4: Node with highest ID that is alive wins, sends COORDINATOR message to all. Messages: O(N^2) in worst case. Advantage: Simple. Disadvantage: High message overhead. Ring Election Algorithm Processes are arranged in a logical ring. Each process knows its neighbour. Ring Election Algorithm SCENARIO: Node

Each process knows its neighbour. Ring Election Algorithm SCENARIO: Node 2 detects leader failure, starts election. [1] --> [2*] --> [3] --> [4] --> [5] --> [6] --> [1] (* = initiator) Step 1: Node 2 sends ELECTION(2) to neighbour Node 3. Step 2: Each node receiving ELECTION(id): if id

Node 3. Step 2: Each node receiving ELECTION(id): if id > own id, forward; else replace with own id. Step 3: Message travels around ring. When it returns to Node 2 with its OWN id => winner. Step 4: Node 2 sends COORDINATOR(2) around ring to announce leadership. Messages: O(N).

2 sends COORDINATOR(2) around ring to announce leadership. Messages: O(N). Advantage: fewer messages. Disadvantage: N messages just to detect winner. Raft Leader Election (Recap) As covered in Chapter 7: uses randomized timeouts (150-300ms), terms, and RequestVote RPCs. Node with most up-to-date log and majority votes wins. Algorithm Message Complexity Assumption
up-to-date log and majority votes wins. Algorithm Message Complexity Assumption Pros Cons

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 22 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes Bully O(N^2) Unique IDs; synchronous Simple to understand High message overhead; 'bully' with high ID always wins Ring O(N) Ring topology; unique IDs Fewer messages

always wins Ring O(N) Ring topology; unique IDs Fewer messages Slow (messages travel full ring) Raft O(N) Majority quorum; log entries Practical, crash-tolerant Complex log comparison for vote eligibility 8.3 Failure Detection Failure detection is the mechanism by which nodes in a distributed system determine whether another node has crashed

in a distributed system determine whether another node has crashed or is unreachable. This is challenging because a slow node looks the same as a crashed node. Heartbeat Protocol  Mechanism: Each node periodically sends a 'heartbeat' message (I'm alive) to its neighbours or a central monitor.  Timeout: If

to its neighbours or a central monitor.  Timeout: If no heartbeat is received within a timeout T, the node is suspected to have failed.  Problem: False positives — a healthy but slow/overloaded node might miss heartbeat deadlines. Phi Accrual Failure Detector (Cassandra) Instead of binary (alive/dead), outputs a

Accrual Failure Detector (Cassandra) Instead of binary (alive/dead), outputs a continuous suspicion value (phi) — higher phi = more likely failed. Application sets its own threshold based on tolerance for false positives. SWIM Protocol (Scalable Weakly-consistent Infection-style Membership) Used in modern systems (Consul, Hashicorp Serf). Instead of pinging the suspected

modern systems (Consul, Hashicorp Serf). Instead of pinging the suspected node directly, a random set of other nodes ping it. This avoids overloading a slow node.  Indirect probing: Node A suspects Node B. A asks Nodes C, D, E to ping B. If none get a response, B is

to ping B. If none get a response, B is suspected failed.  Gossip dissemination: Failure information is spread via gossip protocol — each node periodically shares its membership list with a random neighbour. Gossip Protocol A gossip protocol (epidemic protocol) is a way to disseminate information in a distributed

protocol) is a way to disseminate information in a distributed system:  Each node periodically selects a random neighbour and exchanges its knowledge (membership list, failure info).  Information spreads exponentially fast — like a rumour spreading through a crowd.  Convergence time: O(log N) rounds to reach all N

 Convergence time: O(log N) rounds to reach all N nodes.  Advantages: Highly scalable, resilient to failures, no single point of failure.  Used in: Cassandra (gossip for cluster membership), Amazon S3, Riak.

Advanced System Design | 23CS020 Chitkara University  B.E. CSE 6th Sem Page 24 | Exam: 09 April 2026 | 60 Marks | 60 Minutes CHAPTER ✓ QUICK REVISION SHEET — Exam Ready! One-Liner Facts for MCQs Latency: Time for ONE request. Throughput = requests PER SECOND. Availability formula: Uptime

ONE request. Throughput = requests PER SECOND. Availability formula: Uptime / (Uptime + Downtime). 99.999% = 5.26 min downtime/year. Functional req.: WHAT system does. Non-functional = HOW WELL it does it. Microservices: Independent deployable services, each owning its own data. Distributed system: Independent computers appearing as one; communicate by messages.

Distributed system: Independent computers appearing as one; communicate by messages. No Global Clock: Each node has own clock; use Lamport/Vector clocks for ordering. Lamport rule: L = max(L, received_T) + 1. Captures happened-before, not concurrency. Vector clocks: Can detect CONCURRENT events; Lamport clocks CANNOT. Crash failure: Node stops completely. Byzantine

events; Lamport clocks CANNOT. Crash failure: Node stops completely. Byzantine = node sends WRONG/malicious data. Fault tolerance: Redundancy + Replication + Checkpointing + Circuit Breaker. Mutual exclusion: Only ONE process in critical section at a time. Ricart-Agrawala: 2(N-1) messages; all nodes must OK the request. No central coordinator. Token Ring:

nodes must OK the request. No central coordinator. Token Ring: Only token holder enters CS. Lost token = problem. Deadlock 4 conds.: Mutual Exclusion + Hold&Wait; + No Preemption + Circular Wait. Banker's Algorithm: Deadlock AVOIDANCE — checks safe state before resource allocation. Deadlock Detection: Maintain WFG; cycle in WFG

before resource allocation. Deadlock Detection: Maintain WFG; cycle in WFG = deadlock. Parallel computing: Same machine, shared memory, speed up computation. Distributed comp.: Different machines, message passing, fault tolerance + scale. Amdahl's Law: Speedup = 1/(S + (1-S)/N); limited by sequential fraction S. CAP — C: Consistency: all nodes see

sequential fraction S. CAP — C: Consistency: all nodes see same data. C in CAP != ACID C. CAP — A: Availability: every request gets non-error response. CAP — P: Partition Tolerance: system works despite network splits. CAP choice: Must pick CP or AP (P is mandatory in real distributed

pick CP or AP (P is mandatory in real distributed systems). CP examples: Zookeeper, MongoDB, HBase, CockroachDB. AP examples: Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB, Riak. Eventual consistency: All replicas converge eventually if no new updates. Weakest model. Linearizability: Strongest consistency. Operations appear instantaneous and ordered. PACELC: PAC (partition) + ELC (else: normal

instantaneous and ordered. PACELC: PAC (partition) + ELC (else: normal case latency vs consistency). FLP Impossibility: Cannot guarantee consensus in async system if even 1 node may fail. Paxos roles: Proposer, Acceptor, Learner. Need majority (quorum) to decide. Paxos phases: Phase 1: Prepare/Promise. Phase 2: Accept/Accepted. Raft states: Follower (default)

Phase 1: Prepare/Promise. Phase 2: Accept/Accepted. Raft states: Follower (default) -> Candidate (election) -> Leader. Raft terms: Monotonically increasing. Higher term => more recent. Node with higher term wins. Raft election: Random timeout (150-300ms). Most up-to-date log + majority votes wins. Raft commit: Entry committed when replicated to MAJORITY of

wins. Raft commit: Entry committed when replicated to MAJORITY of nodes. Bully Algorithm: Highest ID node wins. O(N^2) messages. Ring Election: Message circulates ring; highest ID wins. O(N) messages. Heartbeat: Periodic 'I'm alive' message. No heartbeat in timeout = suspected failed.
failed.
